# <!-- block: concepts --> Business summary

Ghostbusters is a professional paranormal investigation and elimination service based in New York City. The company provides rapid-response containment of supernatural entities — ghosts, demons, and other spectral phenomena — for residential, commercial, and governmental clients. Revenue is generated through service fees charged per investigation and containment operation. The business was founded by three former Columbia University parapsychology researchers who leveraged their proprietary nuclear-powered containment technology (the Proton Pack) to create an entirely new industry: commercial paranormal elimination. The brand promise is simple — "Who you gonna call?" — backed by a track record of successful containment and an unmistakable visual identity.

# <!-- block: concepts --> stakeholders

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> Ghostbusters Founders (Venkman, Stantz, Spengler)
Co-founders and principal operators. They hold equal equity, bring complementary expertise (leadership, engineering, theoretical physics), and are the public face of the brand. Their reputations ARE the business.

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> Winston Zeddemore
Fourth team member, hired as field operator. Brings military experience and a pragmatic, everyman perspective. Salaried employee with equity participation.

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> Janine Melnitz
Office manager and first point of contact. Handles scheduling, billing, and client intake. Critical operational role despite the small team.

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> Dana Barrett
High-profile client and romantic interest of Venkman. Her apartment haunting at 550 Central Park West was the catalyst that validated the business model beyond academic curiosity.

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> Mayor of New York City
Government stakeholder who initially suppresses the Ghostbusters but later relies on them during the Gozer crisis. Represents regulatory and political risk/reward.

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> EPA (Walter Peck)
Regulatory antagonist. Represents the risk of government interference with untested technology. His enforcement action at the containment unit nearly destroyed the business.

* <!-- block: Stakeholders --> Gozer the Gozerian
Existential threat stakeholder — the entity whose invasion validated the Ghostbusters' technology at the highest possible stakes.

# <!-- block: concepts --> segments

* <!-- block: Segments --> Residential Hauntings
Homeowners and apartment dwellers experiencing paranormal activity. High emotional distress, willing to pay premium for rapid response. Repeat business unlikely but strong referral potential.

* <!-- block: Segments --> Commercial Properties
Businesses (hotels, offices, warehouses) with supernatural disturbances threatening operations or property value. Larger contracts, ongoing maintenance relationships possible.

* <!-- block: Segments --> Government & Institutional
City, state, or federal agencies dealing with paranormal threats beyond their capability. High-value contracts, bureaucratic procurement process, political dimensions.

* <!-- block: Segments --> Emergency / Crisis Response
Immediate, high-stakes situations involving mass hauntings or interdimensional events. Premium pricing, reputational make-or-break moments.

# <!-- block: concepts --> profiles

* <!-- block: Profiles --> Distressed Homeowner
Middle-class NYC resident experiencing unexplained phenomena in their home. Panicked, skeptical, desperate. Needs reassurance and fast action.

* <!-- block: Profiles --> Property Manager
Manages commercial or residential buildings. Pragmatic, cost-conscious, concerned about tenant complaints and property value. Needs documentation and insurance-friendly reporting.

* <!-- block: Profiles --> City Official
Government employee or elected official. Risk-averse, politically motivated, needs plausible deniability until the threat is confirmed. Once convinced, becomes a powerful ally.

* <!-- block: Profiles --> Paranormal Enthusiast
Amateur ghost hunter or believer who contacts Ghostbusters out of fascination rather than distress. Lower revenue potential but valuable as brand ambassadors and referral sources.

# <!-- block: concepts --> persona

# <!-- block: concepts --> Segmentation

* <!-- block: Segmentation --> Geographic
Primarily New York City metropolitan area. The density of old buildings, long history, and population concentration create ideal conditions for paranormal activity.

* <!-- block: Segmentation --> Urgency Level
Situations range from mild disturbances (flickering lights, cold spots) to full-scale interdimensional breaches. Response protocol and pricing scale with urgency.

* <!-- block: Segmentation --> Entity Type
Class I (residual hauntings) through Class VII (full-torso apparitions) and beyond. Entity classification determines equipment loadout and team composition.

* <!-- block: Segmentation --> Budget Capacity
From individual homeowners paying out-of-pocket to city emergency budgets with unlimited authorization during crises.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Market trends

* <!-- block: Market trends --> Rising Paranormal Activity
The frequency and intensity of paranormal manifestations has been increasing since the early 1980s, driving demand for professional containment services.

* <!-- block: Market trends --> Public Awareness
Media coverage of the Ghostbusters' successes has normalized the concept of hiring paranormal professionals, reducing the stigma of "believing."

* <!-- block: Market trends --> Technology Advancement
Advances in particle physics and containment technology are enabling more effective and portable equipment, expanding the range of addressable threats.

* <!-- block: Market trends --> Regulatory Scrutiny
Government agencies are beginning to take notice of paranormal phenomena, creating both opportunity (government contracts) and risk (regulatory burden).

# <!-- block: concepts --> Market size

The addressable market for paranormal services in New York City is substantial but unquantified by traditional standards. With an estimated 12,000+ haunted locations in the five boroughs (based on historical density of pre-1900 buildings and documented paranormal reports), and assuming a 5% annual manifestation rate, the serviceable market is approximately 600 incidents per year. At an average contract value of $5,000-$15,000, the local TAM ranges from $3M to $9M annually. The Gozer event has expanded awareness nationally, suggesting potential for geographic expansion.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Competition

* <!-- block: Competition --> Independent Exorcists
Religious or spiritual practitioners offering cleansing rituals. Lower technology overhead but limited effectiveness against Class III+ entities. Perceived as unscientific by mainstream clients.

* <!-- block: Competition --> Paranormal Investigation Groups
Amateur ghost-hunting clubs with consumer-grade equipment. No containment capability — they observe and document but cannot eliminate threats.

* <!-- block: Competition --> Government Agencies
FEMA, National Guard, or military units could theoretically address paranormal threats if given political will and appropriate technology. Currently lacks both.

* <!-- block: Competition --> Containment Unit Failure
The Ghostbusters' own containment unit at the firehouse represents a self-inflicted competitive risk. If it fails, all previously captured entities are released simultaneously.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Roles

* <!-- block: Roles --> Decision Maker
The person who authorizes payment — homeowner, property manager, or government official. May not be the person experiencing the haunting.

* <!-- block: Roles --> Influencer
The person who recommends Ghostbusters — often a neighbor, colleague, or media personality who has witnessed a successful operation.

* <!-- block: Roles --> End User
The person living or working in the affected space. Experiences the distress directly and provides access to the location.

* <!-- block: Roles --> Gatekeeper
The person who controls access to the decision maker — administrative assistants, building superintendents, or junior staff who first report the phenomenon.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Problems

* <!-- block: Problems --> Uncontained Paranormal Activity
Spectral entities cause property damage, psychological distress, and in extreme cases physical danger. No effective solution exists outside of Ghostbusters' proprietary technology.

* <!-- block: Problems --> Public Skepticism
Many potential clients dismiss paranormal activity as imagination, structural issues, or hoaxes. This delays engagement and allows situations to escalate.

* <!-- block: Problems --> Lack of Industry Standards
No licensing, certification, or regulatory framework exists for paranormal elimination. Every engagement is a trust exercise.

* <!-- block: Problems --> Equipment Dependency
The Proton Pack and related technology are one-of-a-kind. There is no supply chain, no manufacturer, and no replacement parts pipeline.

* <!-- block: Problems --> Government Interference
Regulatory bodies like the EPA may shut down operations without understanding the technology, potentially releasing contained entities.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Value propositions

* <!-- block: Value propositions --> Rapid Containment
24/7 emergency response with proprietary nuclear-powered containment technology. The only entity in the world capable of capturing and holding Class VII paranormal manifestations.

* <!-- block: Value propositions --> Scientific Credibility
Founded by PhD-level researchers from Columbia University. The team brings academic rigor to a field dominated by superstition and guesswork.

* <!-- block: Value propositions --> Proven Track Record
Successfully contained the Stay Puft Marshmallow Man, defeated Gozer the Gozerian, and cleared hundreds of residential and commercial hauntings.

* <!-- block: Value propositions --> Brand Recognition
"Who you gonna call?" is universally recognized. The Ghostbusters brand is synonymous with paranormal solutions.

* <!-- block: Value propositions --> Insurance Documentation
Professional service reports suitable for insurance claims and property disclosure requirements.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Messages

* <!-- block: Messages --> "Who you gonna call?"
The iconic tagline. Simple, memorable, positions Ghostbusters as the only answer to paranormal problems.

* <!-- block: Messages --> "We're ready to believe you."
Addresses the skepticism barrier directly. Creates a safe space for clients to describe their experience without judgment.

* <!-- block: Messages --> "We came, we saw, we kicked its ass!"
Post-operation victory message that reinforces effectiveness and confidence. Builds brand mythology.

* <!-- block: Messages --> "Don't cross the streams."
Technical limitation communicated as safety protocol. Demonstrates expertise while managing client expectations.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Channels

* <!-- block: Channels --> Word of Mouth
Primary acquisition channel. Successful operations generate referrals among affected communities and social networks.

* <!-- block: Channels --> Media Coverage
Newspaper, TV, and radio coverage of major events (Gozer, Stay Puft) provides massive brand awareness at zero cost.

* <!-- block: Channels --> Direct Response
Phone calls to the firehouse headquarters. The iconic phone number (555-2368) is known across the city.

* <!-- block: Channels --> Government Contracts
Formal procurement process for city, state, and federal engagements. Slower sales cycle but higher contract values.

* <!-- block: Channels --> On-Site Presence
The Ecto-1 vehicle and Ghostbusters uniforms create mobile billboards during every operation.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Relationships

Ghostbusters maintains a direct, high-touch relationship model. Every engagement begins with an on-site assessment and personal consultation. The team's charismatic, humorous communication style disarms skeptical clients and builds trust quickly. Post-operation follow-up is informal but effective — satisfied clients become vocal advocates. The brand personality is confident, irreverent, and reassuring: "We've seen worse, and we can handle this."

# <!-- block: concepts --> Perceptions

* <!-- block: Perceptions --> "They're crazy, but they work."
The dominant perception among potential clients. The Ghostbusters' unconventional methods and personality inspire doubt, but results speak for themselves.

* <!-- block: Perceptions --> "Expensive but worth it."
Clients who have experienced failed attempts by other providers recognize the value of actual containment versus observation.

* <!-- block: Perceptions --> "They're the only ones who can do this."
Market position reinforced by the absence of competitors with comparable technology.

* <!-- block: Perceptions --> "Government should be handling this, not private contractors."
Political perception that creates regulatory risk but also validates the market need.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Emotions

* <!-- block: Emotions --> Fear
The primary emotion driving client acquisition. Fear of the unknown, fear of property damage, fear for family safety.

* <!-- block: Emotions --> Relief
The overwhelming emotion after a successful containment. Clients feel safe for the first time in weeks or months.

* <!-- block: Emotions --> Trust
Built through the team's confidence, scientific explanations, and proven results. Transforms skeptics into advocates.

* <!-- block: Emotions --> Awe
The spectacle of the Ghostbusters' equipment and operations creates a sense of wonder that transcends the fear.

* <!-- block: Emotions --> Gratitude
Deep appreciation for solving a problem that no one else could address. Often expressed as lifelong loyalty and referrals.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Behaviors

* <!-- block: Behaviors --> Initial Contact
Client calls after exhausting other options (priests, contractors, therapists). Usually hesitant and apologetic about "sounding crazy."

* <!-- block: Behaviors --> Referral
Satisfied clients actively recommend Ghostbusters to neighbors, colleagues, and anyone experiencing similar phenomena. The most powerful growth channel.

* <!-- block: Behaviors --> Repeat Engagement
Properties with recurring paranormal activity become ongoing service contracts. Some locations require quarterly containment sweeps.

* <!-- block: Behaviors --> Media Consumption
Clients and prospects follow Ghostbusters coverage in the media, reinforcing brand awareness and trust between engagements.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Journey

* Step 1: Discovery — Client experiences unexplained phenomena and begins searching for solutions
* Step 2: Skepticism — Initial doubt about the reality of the threat and the legitimacy of Ghostbusters
* Step 3: Contact — Client reaches out to Ghostbusters, often apologetically
* Step 4: Assessment — On-site evaluation by the team, scientific explanation of findings
* Step 5: Engagement — Client authorizes the operation
* Step 6: Containment — The Ghostbusters execute the elimination using Proton Packs and containment technology
* Step 7: Resolution — Threat eliminated, client relieved, documentation provided
* Step 8: Advocacy — Client becomes a referral source and brand advocate

# <!-- block: concepts --> Products and services

* <!-- block: Products and services --> Paranormal Investigation
Comprehensive assessment of reported supernatural activity using Ecto-Goggles, PKE Meters, and other diagnostic equipment. Includes classification of entity type and threat level.

* <!-- block: Products and services --> Containment Operations
Full-spectrum elimination of paranormal entities using Proton Packs and portable containment units. The core revenue-generating service.

* <!-- block: Products and services --> Emergency Response
24/7 rapid deployment for acute paranormal crises. Premium pricing for immediate response and high-risk situations.

* <!-- block: Products and services --> Preventive Maintenance
Ongoing monitoring and containment sweeps for properties with recurring paranormal activity. Retainer-based service model.

* <!-- block: Products and services --> Consultation & Assessment
Expert evaluation of paranormal claims for insurance companies, real estate transactions, and legal proceedings.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Portfolio

The Ghostbusters portfolio is structured as a tiered service offering:
- **Tier 1 — Assessment**: Diagnostic evaluation, entity classification, threat assessment. Fixed fee.
- **Tier 2 — Standard Containment**: Single-entity elimination with standard equipment loadout. Per-operation pricing.
- **Tier 3 — Emergency Response**: Multi-entity or high-threat situations requiring full team deployment. Premium pricing with rapid response guarantee.
- **Tier 4 — Enterprise/Institutional**: Ongoing containment contracts for large properties, government facilities, or high-risk locations. Retainer-based.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Components

* <!-- block: Components --> Proton Pack
Portable nuclear accelerator backpack that fires a positively charged particle stream to trap entities. The core technology asset — compact, powerful, and irreplaceable.

* <!-- block: Components --> Containment Unit
Ground-based facility at the firehouse headquarters that stores captured entities in a sub-dimensional matrix. Requires constant power supply and monitoring.

* <!-- block: Components --> Ecto-1
Modified 1959 Cadillac Miller-Meteor ambulance equipped with scanning equipment and transport capability. Serves as mobile operations center and brand billboard.

* <!-- block: Components --> PKE Meter
Psychokinetic Energy detector used for entity tracking and classification. Essential diagnostic tool for every engagement.

* <!-- block: Components --> Ghost Traps
Portable capture devices for smaller entities. Single-use containers that can be transported to the containment unit.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Features

* <!-- block: Features --> Entity Classification System
Proprietary taxonomy (Class I through Class VII+) enabling consistent threat assessment and resource allocation across all engagements.

* <!-- block: Features --> Rapid Deployment
Team can be operational within 30 minutes of receiving a call. Ecto-1 enables fast transit across the city with full equipment loadout.

* <!-- block: Features --> Scientific Documentation
Every engagement produces detailed reports including PKE readings, entity classification, containment methodology, and post-operation verification.

* <!-- block: Features --> 24/7 Availability
Round-the-clock operations with on-call rotation. Paranormal activity doesn't follow business hours.

* <!-- block: Features --> Brand Identity
The Ghostbusters logo, uniform, and vehicle create immediate recognition and trust. A walking, driving billboard for the brand.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Roadmap

* Phase 1: Foundation — Establish operations, prove technology, build initial client base in NYC
* Phase 2: Expansion — Add team members, expand equipment capacity, secure government contracts
* Phase 3: Franchise — License the Ghostbusters brand and methodology to other cities
* Phase 4: Training Academy — Develop formal training program for next-generation Ghostbusters

# <!-- block: concepts --> Pricing

Pricing follows a tiered model based on engagement complexity:
- **Assessment**: $500 fixed fee (applied to containment contract if engaged)
- **Standard Containment**: $5,000-$15,000 per entity (varies by Class)
- **Emergency Response**: $15,000-$50,000 (multi-entity or Class VI+ threats)
- **Retainer Contracts**: $2,000-$10,000/month (depending on property size and threat level)
- **Government Contracts**: Custom pricing per SOW (typically 20-30% premium for compliance overhead)

# <!-- block: concepts --> Offerings

* <!-- block: Offerings --> Ghostbuster Standard
Complete paranormal investigation and containment for residential clients. Includes assessment, single-entity containment, documentation, and 30-day warranty.

* <!-- block: Offerings --> Ghostbuster Commercial
Extended service package for business clients. Includes site assessment, containment, employee briefing, and preventive maintenance schedule.

* <!-- block: Offerings --> Ghostbuster Emergency
Immediate response for acute paranormal crises. Full team deployment with heavy equipment. Priority scheduling, premium pricing.

* <!-- block: Offerings --> Ghostbuster Enterprise
Annual retainer for large properties or government facilities. Includes scheduled sweeps, priority response, and dedicated account management.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Brochure

The Ghostbusters brochure communicates three key messages: (1) We are the only ones who can actually solve your problem, (2) We are scientists, not charlatans, and (3) We have a proven track record. The brochure includes the iconic logo, team photos, equipment descriptions, case studies, and client testimonials. The tone is confident and slightly irreverent — serious about the work, not about ourselves.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Naming

"Ghostbusters" is the brand name — direct, descriptive, and unforgettable. The name was chosen for its clarity: clients immediately understand what the business does. The "-busters" suffix conveys action and authority. Sub-brands include "Ghostbusters II" (the sequel, representing expanded capabilities) and the iconic tagline "Who you gonna call?" which functions as a secondary brand identifier.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Branding

Ghostbusters brand personality: Confident, irreverent, scientific, approachable. The brand promises that no ghost is too big, no job too weird. Tone of voice is casual and humorous but backed by genuine expertise. The brand is approachable — we laugh WITH you, not at you. Brand values: courage, innovation, service, integrity.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Visual identity

The Ghostbusters visual identity is built around the "No Ghosts" logo — a cartoon ghost trapped behind a red prohibition sign. Color palette: red (danger, urgency, action), white (ghosts, the supernatural), black (authority, professionalism). Typography is bold and sans-serif, conveying strength and clarity. The visual language is consistent across all touchpoints: uniforms, vehicle, equipment, and marketing materials.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Logo

The Ghostbusters logo is a white ghost behind a red circle with a diagonal line through it. Created by Dan Aykroyd and designed by artist Michael C. Gross. Usage rules: the logo must always appear on a contrasting background, the ghost must never be modified, and the red prohibition sign must remain intact. The logo works at any size — from business cards to the side of Ecto-1.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Media plan

Media strategy leverages earned media from operations rather than paid advertising. The Gozer event generated massive free coverage. Ongoing media plan includes: press releases for major operations, television news relationships, community outreach events, and the iconic radio jingle ("Ghostbusters" by Ray Parker Jr.) which serves as the primary brand anthem.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Pitch

Ghostbusters solves a problem that no one else can. Paranormal activity is real, it's increasing, and it threatens property, safety, and peace of mind. We are the only team in the world with the technology and expertise to contain it. Our track record speaks for itself — from individual apartments to the Gozer crisis, we deliver results. The question isn't whether you need us. It's "Who you gonna call?"

# <!-- block: concepts --> Web

The Ghostbusters website (ghostbusters.com) serves as the primary digital presence. Key pages: Services (what we do), Emergency (instant contact form), Case Studies (proof of results), Team (credibility building), and Contact (phone, email, location). The site features the iconic jingle on load, real-time availability status, and a live "haunting tracker" for NYC.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Storytelling

The Ghostbusters origin story: Three Columbia University parapsychology professors lose their grant funding and decide to go into business for themselves. They discover that the paranormal is real, the demand is massive, and no one else has the technology to address it. The narrative arc moves from academic curiosity to commercial success to existential crisis (Gozer) and back. It's a story about science, courage, and the underdog.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Presentations

Key presentations include: (1) Investor pitch — market opportunity, technology moat, financial projections; (2) Government briefing — capabilities, safety protocols, compliance framework; (3) Client consultation — problem assessment, proposed solution, pricing; (4) Training deck — methodology, equipment, safety procedures.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Inspiration

* <!-- block: Inspiration --> Academic Research
The founders' parapsychology research at Columbia University revealed that ghosts are scientifically verifiable phenomena, not folklore. The inspiration was realizing that academic research could become a commercial service.

* <!-- block: Inspiration --> Unmet Market Need
Every haunted location in NYC was being addressed with ineffective methods — exorcisms, renovations, or abandonment. The founders saw a gap that only their technology could fill.

* <!-- block: Inspiration --> Government Neglect
The complete absence of government infrastructure for paranormal response meant the private sector had to step in. The founders became that private sector.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Opportunity

* <!-- block: Opportunity --> First Mover Advantage
No other entity in the world has comparable technology or operational capability. Ghostbusters can establish market dominance before competitors emerge.

* <!-- block: Opportunity --> Growing Demand
Paranormal activity frequency is increasing, creating a larger addressable market every year. The Gozer event proved that the threat is real and escalating.

* <!-- block: Opportunity --> Brand Equity
The Ghostbusters brand has achieved universal recognition. The name, logo, and jingle are embedded in popular culture. This brand equity is a durable competitive moat.

* <!-- block: Opportunity --> Government Contracting
The Gozer crisis demonstrated that government agencies will pay premium rates for effective paranormal response. This opens a new, high-value revenue stream.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Business status

Early traction with strong proof of concept. The team has successfully contained hundreds of entities, including the Gozer event (Class VII+). Revenue is growing, brand awareness is at peak levels, and the team is scaling operations. The containment unit at the firehouse is at capacity, requiring expansion. The business is positioned for growth but faces equipment constraints and regulatory headwinds.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Challenges

* <!-- block: Challenges --> Equipment Scalability
Proton Packs are hand-built and irreplaceable. Scaling the team requires scaling equipment production, which is currently a bottleneck.

* <!-- block: Challenges --> Containment Capacity
The firehouse containment unit is at maximum capacity. Expansion requires significant capital investment and engineering.

* <!-- block: Challenges --> Regulatory Risk
The EPA and other agencies may impose restrictions on nuclear-powered equipment, potentially crippling operations.

* <!-- block: Challenges --> Talent Acquisition
Finding qualified personnel who can operate Proton Packs and handle the psychological demands of paranormal combat is extremely difficult.

* <!-- block: Challenges --> Brand Management
The team's unconventional personality (especially Venkman's) creates media risks that could damage the brand.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Mission

To protect New York City — and eventually the world — from paranormal threats through the application of proprietary technology, scientific expertise, and unwavering courage. We believe every person deserves to live free from supernatural fear.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Vision

A world where no one has to face the paranormal alone. A future where Ghostbusters technology is deployed globally, where containment infrastructure is standard in every major city, and where the paranormal is managed as effectively as any other natural phenomenon.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Organizational values

* <!-- block: Organizational values --> Science First
Every decision is grounded in evidence and expertise. We don't guess — we measure, classify, and respond with precision.

* <!-- block: Organizational values --> Courage
We run toward what others run from. Fear is natural; paralysis is not an option.

* <!-- block: Organizational values --> Service
We exist to protect people. Every engagement is a responsibility, not just a job.

* <!-- block: Organizational values --> Integrity
We tell clients the truth — even when it's uncomfortable. We don't overstate threats or understate risks.

* <!-- block: Organizational values --> Innovation
We constantly improve our technology and methodology. The paranormal evolves; so must we.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Organizational goals

* <!-- block: Organizational goals --> 100% Containment Rate
Maintain a perfect success rate for entity containment across all engagement types. No entity escapes once captured.

* <!-- block: Organizational goals --> Equipment Scaling
Develop manufacturing capability for Proton Packs and Ghost Traps to support team expansion to 20+ operators by Year 3.

* <!-- block: Organizational goals --> Containment Expansion
Expand the containment facility to 10x current capacity within 18 months.

* <!-- block: Organizational goals --> Geographic Expansion
Launch Ghostbusters operations in at least 5 additional major US cities within 5 years through a franchise model.

* <!-- block: Organizational goals --> Government Partnerships
Secure formal contracts with FEMA, the Department of Defense, and at least 3 state governments.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Activities

* <!-- block: Activities --> Paranormal Response
The core operational activity: receiving calls, deploying teams, assessing threats, and executing containment operations. Runs 24/7.

* <!-- block: Activities --> Equipment Maintenance
Continuous maintenance, calibration, and repair of Proton Packs, PKE Meters, Ecto-1, and containment systems. Critical for operational readiness.

* <!-- block: Activities --> Training & Development
Ongoing skill development for all team members. Includes new equipment training, entity classification updates, and safety protocol reviews.

* <!-- block: Activities --> Client Communication
Pre-engagement consultations, during-operation updates, and post-engagement follow-up. Client satisfaction drives referrals.

* <!-- block: Activities --> Research & Development
Continuous improvement of containment technology, entity classification methodology, and operational procedures.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Functions

* <!-- block: Functions --> Field Operations
Direct client engagement — investigation, containment, and resolution. The revenue-generating function.

* <!-- block: Functions --> Technical Operations
Equipment design, manufacturing, maintenance, and containment unit management. The technology backbone.

* <!-- block: Functions --> Client Services
Intake, scheduling, billing, and post-engagement support. The client-facing operational function.

* <!-- block: Functions --> Business Development
Government contracting, partnership development, and franchise expansion. The growth function.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Resources

* <!-- block: Resources --> Proton Packs
The core technology asset. Each pack is hand-built, irreplaceable, and worth more than the firehouse itself. Currently 4 operational units.

* <!-- block: Resources --> Containment Unit
The facility that stores captured entities. Requires constant power and monitoring. Located at the firehouse headquarters.

* <!-- block: Resources --> Ecto-1
The Ghostbusters vehicle. Equipped with scanning equipment, transport capability, and serving as a mobile operations center. Iconic brand asset.

* <!-- block: Resources --> Firehouse HQ
Headquarters, laboratory, and containment facility. A decommissioned NYC firehouse repurposed for paranormal operations.

* <!-- block: Resources --> PKE Meters & Diagnostic Equipment
Essential tools for entity detection, tracking, and classification. Multiple units available but must be maintained to precision standards.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Goals

* <!-- block: Goals --> Venkman: Operational Excellence
Lead the team to maintain 100% containment rate while scaling operations. Focus on client relationships and brand management.

* <!-- block: Goals --> Stantz: Technology Development
Advance Proton Pack technology and develop scalable manufacturing processes. Lead R&D efforts.

* <!-- block: Goals --> Spengler: Scientific Advancement
Deepen understanding of paranormal phenomena. Develop new classification systems and containment methodologies.

* <!-- block: Goals --> Zeddemore: Operational Efficiency
Streamline field operations and team coordination. Ensure every engagement runs smoothly from deployment to resolution.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Skills

* <!-- block: Skills --> Paranormal Physics
Deep understanding of psychokinetic energy, spectral manifestation, and containment theory. PhD-level expertise.

* <!-- block: Skills --> Equipment Operation
Proficiency with Proton Packs, PKE Meters, and containment systems. Requires extensive training and physical conditioning.

* <!-- block: Skills --> Entity Classification
Ability to identify and classify paranormal entities by type, threat level, and containment requirements.

* <!-- block: Skills --> Client Communication
Ability to explain paranormal phenomena in scientific yet accessible terms. Builds trust with skeptical clients.

* <!-- block: Skills --> Crisis Management
Rapid decision-making under extreme pressure. The ability to adapt when containment operations don't go as planned.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Contributions

* <!-- block: Contributions --> Venkman: Leadership & Client Relations
Drives client acquisition through charisma and confidence. The public face of the brand.

* <!-- block: Contributions --> Stantz: Equipment & Innovation
Built the first functional Proton Pack. Continuously improves equipment design and maintenance protocols.

* <!-- block: Contributions --> Spengler: Theory & Methodology
Developed the entity classification system and containment theory. The scientific mind behind the operations.

* <!-- block: Contributions --> Zeddemore: Field Operations & Ground Truth
Brings practical, real-world perspective. Ensures operations are executed efficiently and safely.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Compensations

* <!-- block: Compensations --> Equal Equity Split
The four founders share equity equally. Profits are distributed based on ownership percentage after operational costs.

* <!-- block: Compensations --> Performance Bonuses
Team members receive bonuses based on containment success rate, client satisfaction, and revenue targets.

* <!-- block: Compensations --> Equipment Access
All team members have full access to Ghostbusters equipment and training. Professional development is built into the compensation model.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Positions

* <!-- block: Positions --> Co-Founder / Lead Investigator (Venkman)
Primary client-facing role. Leads field operations, manages client relationships, and serves as team spokesperson.

* <!-- block: Positions --> Co-Founder / Technical Director (Stantz)
Leads equipment development, maintenance, and R&D. Responsible for operational readiness of all technology assets.

* <!-- block: Positions --> Co-Founder / Chief Scientist (Spengler)
Leads theoretical research, entity classification, and methodology development. Responsible for scientific integrity.

* <!-- block: Positions --> Field Operator (Zeddemore)
Executes field operations under team leadership. Provides ground-level expertise and operational support.

* <!-- block: Positions --> Office Manager (Melnitz)
Manages scheduling, billing, client intake, and administrative operations. The organizational backbone.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Phases

* Phase 1: Startup — Found the company, acquire first clients, prove technology in the field
* Phase 2: Growth — Scale team, expand equipment capacity, secure government contracts
* Phase 3: Expansion — Launch franchise model, enter new markets, build training academy
* Phase 4: Maturity — Establish industry standards, develop next-generation technology, build global presence

# <!-- block: concepts --> Milestones

* 1984-Q1: First commercial containment operation (Shandor Building)
* 1984-Q2: Firehouse headquarters operational with containment unit
* 1984-Q3: Ecto-1 deployed, brand identity established
* 1984-Q4: Gozer event — largest containment operation in history
* 1985-Q1: Containment unit expansion completed
* 1985-Q2: First government contract secured (NYC Emergency Management)
* 1985-Q3: Team expansion to 6 operators
* 1985-Q4: Franchise model development complete

# <!-- block: concepts --> Metrics

* <!-- block: Metrics --> Containment Success Rate
Percentage of engagements resulting in successful entity containment. Target: 100%. Currently: 100%.

* <!-- block: Metrics --> Response Time
Average time from client call to on-site deployment. Target: < 30 minutes. Currently: ~25 minutes.

* <!-- block: Metrics --> Client Satisfaction Score
Post-engagement client satisfaction rating. Target: 4.8/5.0. Currently: 4.9/5.0.

* <!-- block: Metrics --> Revenue Per Engagement
Average revenue generated per containment operation. Target: $10,000. Currently: $8,500.

* <!-- block: Metrics --> Referral Rate
Percentage of new clients acquired through referrals. Target: 60%. Currently: 75%.

* <!-- block: Metrics --> Containment Unit Utilization
Percentage of containment unit capacity in use. Target: < 80%. Currently: 95% (critical).

# <!-- block: concepts --> Revenue

* <!-- block: Revenue --> Residential Containment Fees
Per-operation fees for residential haunting elimination. Average: $8,000 per engagement. Volume: ~200 engagements/year.

* <!-- block: Revenue --> Commercial Containment Fees
Per-operation fees for commercial property services. Average: $15,000 per engagement. Volume: ~50 engagements/year.

* <!-- block: Revenue --> Emergency Response Fees
Premium pricing for acute paranormal crises. Average: $30,000 per engagement. Volume: ~10 engagements/year.

* <!-- block: Revenue --> Retainer Contracts
Monthly fees for ongoing preventive maintenance. Average: $5,000/month. Volume: ~15 active contracts.

* <!-- block: Revenue --> Government Contracts
Annual contracts for municipal and federal services. Average: $500,000/year. Volume: 2 active contracts.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Life Time Value

Estimated LTV per residential client: $12,000 (based on 1.5 engagements average, $8,000 per engagement, plus referral value of 0.5 additional clients). Commercial LTV: $75,000 (based on 5-year contract at $15,000/year average). Government LTV: $2,000,000+ (multi-year contracts with expansion potential).

# <!-- block: concepts --> Costs

* <!-- block: Costs --> Equipment (Fixed)
Proton Pack manufacturing and maintenance: $200,000/year. Containment unit power and maintenance: $150,000/year. Ecto-1 operations: $30,000/year.

* <!-- block: Costs --> Personnel (Fixed)
4 founders (profit-sharing): variable. 1 office manager: $45,000/year. 2 additional operators: $60,000/year each.

* <!-- block: Costs --> Operations (Variable)
Fuel, travel, supplies, insurance: ~$500/engagement. Annual total: ~$150,000.

* <!-- block: Costs --> Legal & Compliance
Legal counsel, regulatory compliance, insurance premiums: $100,000/year.

* <!-- block: Costs --> Marketing (Minimal)
Word-of-mouth driven. Minimal paid advertising. Annual: $10,000.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Customer Aquisition Cost

Estimated CAC: $200 per client (based on $10,000 annual marketing spend divided by 50 new clients/month). This is exceptionally low due to the strength of word-of-mouth referrals and media coverage. The Gozer event generated an estimated $2M+ in equivalent advertising value at zero cost.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Unit economics

Per engagement average:
- Revenue: $10,000
- Variable cost: $500 (travel, supplies)
- Contribution margin: $9,500 (95%)
- Fixed cost allocation: ~$2,000
- Net margin per engagement: ~$7,500 (75%)

The business has exceptional unit economics due to the absence of traditional marketing costs and the high perceived value of the service.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Funding sources

* <!-- block: Funding sources --> Founder Investment
Initial capitalization from the three co-founders' personal savings and university severance packages. Estimated: $50,000.

* <!-- block: Funding sources --> Revenue Reinvestment
The business is self-funding from Day 1. All growth is funded through operational cash flow. No external debt or equity financing to date.

* <!-- block: Funding sources --> Government Grants
Potential for DOE or DOD research grants for paranormal containment technology development. Not yet pursued.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Shareholders

* <!-- block: Shareholders --> Peter Venkman (33.3%)
Co-founder, lead investigator, and public face of the brand. Equal equity stake.

* <!-- block: Shareholders --> Ray Stantz (33.3%)
Co-founder and technical director. Equal equity stake.

* <!-- block: Shareholders --> Egon Spengler (33.3%)
Co-founder and chief scientist. Equal equity stake.

Note: Winston Zeddemore and Janine Melnitz participate in profit-sharing but do not hold equity.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Projections

* <!-- block: Projections --> Year 1 Revenue
$2.5M (residential: $1.6M, commercial: $750K, emergency: $300K, retainers: $900K, government: $500K — with overlap and timing adjustments).

* <!-- block: Projections --> Year 2 Revenue
$4.0M (20% volume growth, 15% price increase, 2 new government contracts).

* <!-- block: Projections --> Year 3 Revenue
$7.5M (franchise revenue from 2 additional cities, expanded NYC operations, new government contracts).

# <!-- block: concepts --> Legal issues

* <!-- block: Legal issues --> Nuclear Regulatory Compliance
Proton Packs use nuclear technology. NRC oversight could impose restrictions or require licensing. Currently operating under a grandfather clause that may not survive scrutiny.

* <!-- block: Legal issues --> Liability Insurance
Standard liability policies don't cover "paranormal damage." Custom insurance agreements are in place but may be challenged in court.

* <!-- block: Legal issues --> EPA Enforcement
Walter Peck's EPA investigation represents an ongoing legal threat. The agency could seek injunctions against containment operations.

* <!-- block: Legal issues --> Intellectual Property
Proton Pack technology is proprietary but not formally patented. Risk of reverse-engineering or industrial espionage.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Contracts

* <!-- block: Contracts --> NYC Emergency Management Contract
Annual retainer for paranormal emergency response services. $500,000/year, 3-year term. Includes SLA for response time and containment guarantees.

* <!-- block: Contracts --> Residential Service Agreement
Standard client engagement contract. Includes scope of work, pricing, liability waiver, and 30-day containment warranty.

* <!-- block: Contracts --> Equipment Lease Agreement
Agreement with Columbia University for continued access to laboratory facilities and research equipment. In-kind contribution for ongoing academic collaboration.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Assumptions

* <!-- block: Assumptions --> Paranormal Activity Will Continue Increasing
The historical trend of increasing paranormal manifestations will continue, driving sustained demand growth.

* <!-- block: Assumptions --> No Competitor Will Replicate Our Technology
The technical complexity of Proton Pack technology creates a 5+ year barrier to entry for potential competitors.

* <!-- block: Assumptions --> Government Will Eventually Formalize Paranormal Response
Regulatory frameworks will emerge, and Ghostbusters is positioned to shape and comply with them.

* <!-- block: Assumptions --> Brand Equity Will Sustain Growth
The Ghostbusters brand will remain relevant and trusted as the company scales.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Risks

* <!-- block: Risks --> Containment Unit Failure
If the containment unit loses power or reaches capacity, all captured entities are released simultaneously. This is an existential risk.

* <!-- block: Risks --> Technology Obsolescence
A breakthrough in paranormal science could render Proton Pack technology obsolete. Low probability but high impact.

* <!-- block: Risks --> Regulatory Shutdown
Government intervention could force operations to cease, either temporarily (during compliance review) or permanently (if technology is banned).

* <!-- block: Risks --> Key Person Dependency
The business is entirely dependent on the four founders. Loss of any founder would severely impact operations and brand.

* <!-- block: Risks --> Reputational Damage
A high-profile containment failure or client lawsuit could damage the brand irreparably.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Suggestions

* <!-- block: Suggestions --> Develop Backup Containment Systems
Invest in redundant containment technology to mitigate the single-point-of-failure risk.

* <!-- block: Suggestions --> Formalize Training Program
Create a structured training curriculum to reduce key-person dependency and enable scaling.

* <!-- block: Suggestions --> Pursue Patent Protection
File patents on Proton Pack technology and containment methodology to protect the competitive moat.

* <!-- block: Suggestions --> Engage Proactively with Regulators
Rather than waiting for EPA enforcement, proactively engage with regulatory bodies to shape compliance frameworks.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Unfair advantage

The Ghostbusters' unfair advantage is a combination of irreplicable technology (Proton Packs), operational expertise (the only team in the world with real-world containment experience), and brand equity ("Who you gonna call?" is universally recognized). A competitor would need to simultaneously solve the physics problem, build the equipment, train the team, and establish the brand — a 5-10 year undertaking with no guarantee of success.

# <!-- block: concepts --> SWOT

**Strengths**: Proprietary technology, proven track record, iconic brand, scientific credibility, first-mover advantage, strong team chemistry.

**Weaknesses**: Equipment scalability constraints, key-person dependency, single containment facility, limited geographic coverage, minimal formal business infrastructure.

**Opportunities**: Growing paranormal activity, government contracting, franchise expansion, technology licensing, training academy, international markets.

**Threats**: Regulatory intervention, containment unit failure, competitor emergence, reputational damage, key-person loss, technology obsolescence.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Keys

* <!-- block: Keys --> Containment Technology
The Proton Pack is the foundational technology asset. Without it, the business cannot operate. Protecting and scaling this technology is the single most important priority.

* <!-- block: Keys --> Team Chemistry
The complementary skills and personalities of the four founders create a team greater than the sum of its parts. Maintaining this dynamic is critical.

* <!-- block: Keys --> Brand Trust
The Ghostbusters brand is built on results. One major failure could undermine decades of credibility. Operational excellence is non-negotiable.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Coherence

* <!-- block: Coherence --> Mission vs. Operations
The mission (protect people from paranormal threats) is directly served by operations (containment engagements). Strong alignment.

* <!-- block: Coherence --> Pricing vs. Value
Pricing reflects the unique value proposition — no one else can do this. Clients pay for actual results, not promises. Strong alignment.

* <!-- block: Coherence --> Brand vs. Reality
The irreverent brand personality matches the team's actual communication style. Clients know what to expect. Strong alignment.

* <!-- block: Coherence --> Growth vs. Capacity
The desire to scale conflicts with equipment and facility constraints. This is the primary coherence gap that must be addressed.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Experiments

* <!-- block: Experiments --> Franchise Pilot
Test the franchise model in Chicago by licensing the Ghostbusters brand, methodology, and providing equipment. Measure: containment success rate, client satisfaction, revenue per engagement.

* <!-- block: Experiments --> Government Contract Pilot
Expand the NYC Emergency Management contract to include federal agencies. Measure: contract value, response time, containment success rate under government oversight.

* <!-- block: Experiments --> Equipment Scaling Pilot
Develop a simplified Proton Pack variant for field operators (vs. the full-spec founders' packs). Measure: manufacturing cost, reliability, containment effectiveness.

# <!-- block: concepts --> Misc

The Ghostbusters story is a masterclass in turning academic research into a commercial enterprise. The team leveraged their unique expertise, built a brand that transcended their industry, and created a business model that is simultaneously essential, entertaining, and economically viable. The Gozer event was both the greatest threat and the greatest marketing event in the company's history.
